steps.title: Identify the paradox and what it implies
steps.content: Males prefer green females for mating, yet 28% of adult females remain blue. If mate preference alone drove coloration, blue should have nearly disappeared. The team's hypothesis must explain how blue persists — pointing to a non-mating advantage. The key clue is that males show less antagonism toward blue females in resource competition, giving blue females a survival edge.

steps.title: Eliminate choices that don't fit the logic
steps.content:
• Choice A — the percentage of blue-feathered females will increase until fewer than half of adult female jacobins are green-feathered.: Predicts blue will overtake green — contradicts the passage, which says males prefer green mates, exerting pressure against blue increasing. ✗
  • Choice B — the occurrence of blue head plumage in adult female jacobins is driven by one or more factors not associated with mate attraction.: Matches the inference exactly: reduced male antagonism (a resource-competition advantage) maintains blue despite mate-preference pressure. ✓
  • Choice C — coloration prevents green-feathered adult female jacobins from distinguishing between adult males and blue-feathered adult females.: Introduces a perceptual claim about green females that is unsupported by the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — adult male jacobins do not act antagonistically toward juvenile jacobins with blue head plumage when competing for resources.: Addresses juveniles, not adult blue females — doesn't explain why adult blue females persist. ✗
That is the correct answer.
